item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is probably the article that has scared me the most this one has stuck with me for a long time good article +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lucky this is the first SCP I read. Since then I've read lots and lots on the wiki. Great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was recommended this one from a co-worker. Was a good read, definitely creepy from start to finish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a very good article! I liked it and it really got me claustrophobic! +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Translated into Russian. Great article. I liked how you worked with the metaphor of being buried under your childish memories and self-destruction without making it too obviuos and pretentious and staying in the realm of good old horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the best SCPs I've read in a while. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interestingly enough, at first I expected the ending of this to be more straight forward and kinda in-your-face in terms of creepiness, but it turned out to be more subtle, which I think is even better. The narrative is great too, the story takes some time to build up but never feels like it's going for way too long.

Upvoted furiously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dont think i have ever rooted for a D class before.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A recent comment brought me back here; this is one of my favorites. Pure visceral horror. I remember reading the log being sure that the D-class was going to dig their way to the surface, just to find themself in the barren landscape depicted in the drawing. Which would have been fantastically awful, if maybe too predictable. Either way, amazing work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wow this is very disturbing. you just knew D-6042 was in for an awful time and you didn't spare us from the awful details (whimpering and such). It has an extra layer of disturbance; after this exploration log, the Foundation must think on how everyone who has been lost to this SCP probably suffered a similar fate…creepiest thing I've read on here in a while.

+ show block
– hide block

There was one sour note, it was when D-6042 saw their own face. I think that was a bit too-heavy handed for some effect and it would have been more eerie if we as the reader were left with D-6042's conviction.

This is one of my favs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been a hardcore fan of psul's work for a long time. This is no exception.

I keep feeling that this sends you to the SCP-1562 place, which definitely deserves a further chapter. And this is an exceptionally well done exploration, with twists to build on the characters developed and the scenery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 because of how this just plain averts the site trope where the doctors are detached/just plain mean to the D-class. Not only did it humanize the D-class, it also humanized Mr. doctor dude a whole lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks - I'm pleased you liked that aspect. Fun fact: the "elapsed time" references are part of reinforcing that relationship, by showing that it has developed over time. You'll notice that the D-class eventually uses the doctor's first name, although the doctor limits their lapse in clinical detachment to "buddy" (not least so that I could keep the D-class as easy to identify with as possible).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Plus one for having the single best doctor and D-class communication on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
creepiest fucking scp ive read in a bit
nice long story
and a safe class to boot

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite excellent. No gimmicks, just damn good writing. Really well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hi. +1, but i corrected an instance where you wrote "4062" instead of "6042" between logs 1 and 2. I assume that was not intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Hallard: Could you please go a little nearer so we can see it close up?

Could this be changed to “Could you please get a closer look at the painting?” or something similar?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel bad for missing out on this one, because it's excellent! A great example of how to write D-Class and researchers communicating. It won't always be this smooth, but this kind of thing is rare enough to read anyway that I immensely enjoyed it. I could see the twist coming at the end, but that wasn't an issue when the whole thing kept me gripped anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rarely feel bad for class D's but this is one time I do. Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great job. Truly amazing. I love these horrifying SCP's that make everything they do to stop it seem useless. Plus, the conversations were amazing. It's not often that people actually remember the researchers are people too. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how the claustrophobic atmosphere just builds and builds. Fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: D-6042 was digging his own grave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still in love with the atmosphere and personal horror of this. Super proud to have illustrated it! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A majority of my nightmares, inexplicably, take place in the house I lived in from 1987 to 1997. It wasn't the first place that I have lived in, it wasn't the longest I have lived in the same place, I come from a stable loving family, and nothing more traumatic than ordinary white male suburban adolescence occurred to me there. That this place is the principle setting of terror for my subconscious (and not say, the killing streets of Ar Ramadi), is in and of itself disturbing in its incongruity. That D-6042 returns to that house as well hit me fairly close to where fear lives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your mastery of atmosphere is literally terrific and this is the best horror story that I have read this year. I think that you may very well be the best author from that wave of good authors that all started about the time I did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a conscious stylistic choice. I want to have the logs sitting there from the start, and for the reader to dig deeper and deeper down into them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed how human the interactions between the doctor and D-class were

I'm pleased about this. I figured that spending hundreds of hours with no-one else to talk to, there would be a level of humanisation happening. It was designed to build reader sympathy for D-6042, as you suggest. My other (slightly contradictory) aim with D-6042 was to make them a cipher, a blank into which the reader can imagine themselves. As a result I deliberately avoided giving them a gender, a name or a nationality, their dialogue intentionally skews quite young (to mimic site readership) and I've tried (within reason) to make their house somewhat generic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of TheGreatHippo, in a good way.

Thanks - I take that as a huge compliment.

To Hippo, I owe the suggestion of finding roots in the earth. Until then, there was going to be nothing but dead earth and bones, but the roots are useful for adding a little bit of variety to the weirdness. Hippo was also the one that suggested including the Foundation dig team giving up near the ending, to emphasise the source of the remaining digging noise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: May I Suggest making the audio logs collapse-able to save some space?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man. This reminds me, in a really, really good way, of the slide that sends you head-first into a hole packed with people. As long as it is, those logs are excellent, providing outstanding human interaction and great tension and horror with zero jumpscares or even monsters. I'm not sure I've ever felt this sorry for a D-Class in this history of this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my gosh you actually did it. Hearty +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who is fascinated by sleep and dreams, and moderately phobic about tight enclosed spaces, I found this utterly terrifying. Things What Kill You are weaksauce compared to the blind unreasoning terror of being trapped where you can't get out and no one can get to you.

Garnish liberally with rotting corpses and serve it forth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this reminds me of a life flashing before a person’s eyes, at the moment of their death (something like that). Might the house represent some of the memories of the D-class? Man!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember seeing this on the drafts forum - and thus reading the whole article. Liked it then, like it now. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is superbly creepy. I really enjoyed how human the interactions between the doctor and D-class were, with stuff like asking about the food he'd want after getting out, which gave the D-class's failure and death at the end more impact.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, 2571, probably more I've missed, this site has singlehandedly given me an intense fear of trees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The SCP is a strong idea, i love the use of the tree looking like whomping willow from Harry Potter. The image is great including the actual structure of the logs/ its precise and accurate. Also the tree is good use as a anomalous tree painting. well being as a SCP. The SCP painting looks accurate and a actual sketch with shading is well artistic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of TheGreatHippo, in a good way. Which is not that surprising since he critiqued it.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Trees hate you... TREES HATE YOU

This nicely combines claustrophobia with an unnerving WTF factor. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I didn't have Taphophobia before reading this.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're correct that the Whomping Willow from Harry Potter is also a knotted (or pollarded) willow tree. This is not intended to be precisely the same tree, but it's certainly the same type of tree.

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
Another horror SCP involving underground spaces and the vulnerability of sleep. I wonder at what stage writing on this site becomes too personally revealing?

Many thanks are owed:

  • firstly, to Zhange, who created the rather excellent image used here. Zhange not only worked essentially to (unpaid) commission, but then edited the image when I got picky, and put up with me editing it further. Thank you very much, Zhange - it was great working with you.
  • to Mendelssohn, who also drew an image for potential use on the article. I'm really sorry that I couldn't end up using it.
  • to Captain Kirby, The Great Hippo, and especially jabyrwock and Hercules Rockefeller, for some terrific and detailed feedback on the forums. I didn't use all of it, but it made me think a lot about what I was doing, and I think that helped the end product.
  • to Dr Bleep, for convincing me that the idea was worth pursuing when it was only just formed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't that the tree from harry potter?
